Second, while we had reason to believe prior to the study that $R$ (preregistration) and $S$ (high sample size) might increase the replication rate, there was no variation in these variables in the study. As a consequence, our priors are effectively unchanged without some comparison group that had lower sample sizes or did not implement preregistrations. The `elicitcausal` app as a result records zero causal learning, as shown in the table it produced below:

For the non-preregistered causal variables sample size $S$ and preregistation $R$, I include relatively strong priors of $Pr(0.4)$. Again, with a binary operationalization, these priors imply that the use of a large sample $S=1$ or a quality preregistration $R=1$ should reduce deflation in replicated treatment effects $Y=1$. This prior reflects the researchers' experiences with open science practices and their beliefs that these practices should be efficacious at achieving a higher replication rate (which they later claimed in the published study). However, the prior is not as strong as for the observer effect: there is uncertainty as to how much open science practices increase replicability in a metascientific sense; that is a large part of the reason that the study received so much attention after it was published.

Given these priors, it is straightforward to calculate entropy of the causal graph. Entropy functions are available in most statistical software packages, and I provide additional detail in the appendix for how to define causal graphs and entropy calculations with available packages in R such as `causalqueries` , `dagitty` and `theorytools`. Furthermore, while deriving some of the conditional probability distributions for complex causal graphs can be challenging, large language model coding tools are able to derive these calculations quite easily, as I show below.

As mentioned previously, the study results showed that effect deflation, $Y=1$, did not in fact occur. Replicated effects were similar to original effects. These results match the researchers' priors on the $O \rightarrow Y$ relationship specifically as it was influenced by the experimental treatments $B$ blinding and $N$ repetition of studies over time. We could put an even lower prior on the $O \rightarrow Y$ relationship of $0.1$, though again, we could not update the $B \rightarrow O$ or $N \rightarrow O$ paths given that we do not have any specific measurements of the observer effect. That is, theoretically speaking, our treatments could have influenced this parapsychological construct, but even if they in fact did so, it would not matter as this construct does not seem to have any effect on the outcome.

Given the very limited range of opportunity for causal learning, the `elicitcausal` app revealed an entropy difference of zero--no causal learning--as shown in @tbl-prereg-learn.

::: {#tbl-prereg-learn}
| Node      | Pre entropy (%) | Post entropy (%) | Delta (%) | Direction | Learning Type |
|:-----------|-----------:|-----------:|-----------:|:-----------|:-----------|
| B         |           69.66 |            69.66 |         0 | no change | No Learning   |
| L         |           69.66 |            69.66 |         0 | no change | No Learning   |
| N         |           69.66 |            69.66 |         0 | no change | No Learning   |
| R         |           69.66 |            69.66 |         0 | no change | No Learning   |
| S         |           69.66 |            69.66 |         0 | no change | No Learning   |
| O         |           69.66 |            69.66 |         0 | no change | No Learning   |
| Y         |           43.93 |            43.93 |         0 | no change | No Learning   |
| \[Joint\] |          437.11 |           437.11 |         0 | no change | No Learning   |

: Pre/Post Entropy Changes (Learning) in Protzko et al.
:::

However, as noted by Bak-Coleman and Devezer, the main aim of the paper was to establish new causal knowledge about two variables in that were not preregistered; namely, whether large sample sizes $S$ and quality preregistrations for each lab's study $R$ led to a stable replication rate $Y=0$. Unfortunately, we cannot evaluate any changes in entropy because, as Bak-Coleman and Devezer noted, there is no variation in these independent variables. In fact, it is irrelevant as to whether these variables were preregistered or not given that there is no variation with which to test them. Explicitly incorporating these variables in the causal graph makes this very clear.

## Discussion

At the same time, it is important to note that it is quite plausible that such variation could be created by either performing additional experiments in with $S$ and $R$ were modified in a controlled setting *or* by using external data about replication rates when $S$ was lower or $R$ did not exist. Of course, adding such data would lead to potential selection effects in that the data might not be comparable as Bak-Coleman and Devezer note. Nonetheless, it is important to note that the researchers could well be right that their study shows that open science practices are effective--they just need a comparison that they can draw relative to the data they collected.

It is also possible to think of the study as providing additional evidence for the null effect of observing research studies on the replication rate. In the form presented, the probabilities are provided as discrete numbers; it is possible to also add uncertainty in researcher priors via the Dirichlet distribution [@humphreys2023]. I leave this possibility for future research as it would significantly complicate the construction of causal graphs–it might be better to first begin the process of preregistering graphs before we expand into more complex graph construction.

# Conclusion

<!-- I aver that many of our problems with defining and supporting descriptive research stems from our conception of causality as a binary process: either a piece of research is or is not causal. It is not that RCTs have more issues than are commonly acknowledged, but rather that the definition of RCT as the gold standard means that we artificially deflate the value of other modes of causal analysis. We should take a charitable approach by admitting that various theories and practices of inference contain varying amounts of causal knowledge, with the amount varying in relation to the credibility of the research design. However, as the maximum entropy principle shows, we cannot evaluate the relative causal knowledge obtained without a consideration of what is and is not known about a given causal system. -->

This method of causal graph preregistration can help diversify preregistration from a purely falsificationist standard to a more holistic and flexible form of preregistering our general state of knowledge of a causal process. This method can also be integrated into meta-analysis studies, especially if studies are explicit about the causal graph they seek to understand. Ultimately, the aim of the framework is to create a level playing field for diverse research designs—including both exploratory and confirmatory studies—so that we do not under-invest in important lines of inquiry that might yield important information about causal systems even if they were not included in the original study design.

\newpage

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
